draft.introduction: Book 10 of the Bhagavata Purana narrates the birth and childhood of Krishna—the supreme deity born as a cowherd boy in Vrindavan. His divine play (līlā) reveals the nature of God as joy.

translation.text: Shuka said: When the earth, overburdened by the armies of demons disguised as kings, approached Brahma for relief, Brahma prayed to Vishnu. The Lord consented to descend and was born as the son of Vasudeva and Devaki in the prison of the tyrant Kamsa.
translation.original_text: śrī śuka uvāca | prajāpatiḥ sa bhū-bhāraṃ

translation.text: At the moment of Krishna’s birth, the chains fell from Vasudeva’s hands, the prison doors opened of their own accord, and the guards fell into deep sleep. Vasudeva carried the infant across the Yamuna, which parted to let them pass.
translation.original_text: vasudevaḥ sutaṃ ādāya | baddhaḥ mocanaṃ bandhanaṃ

translator_notes.note: The avatāra doctrine—God descending into human form—is structurally parallel to the Christian Incarnation but functionally different. In Hinduism, God descends repeatedly whenever dharma declines. In Christianity, the Incarnation is a singular, unrepeatable event.
